content.text: An enterprise Claude maturity model answers the question every CoE Director and executive sponsor must confront: where are we, really? A maturity model provides a structured, honest assessment of the organization's current state across the dimensions that determine whether AI programs succeed. Without this assessment, investment decisions, phasing plans, and organizational change efforts are based on intuition rather than evidence.

This document defines the six dimensions of Claude adoption maturity, the four levels within each dimension, the assessment rubric for scoring each dimension, and how to use the results to prioritize investment.

attrs.alt: Claude adoption maturity — six dimensions across four levels

content.text: Claude adoption maturity is a multi-dimensional construct. An organization that has 30 use cases in production but no data governance is not more mature than one with 5 well-governed use cases — it is mature on one dimension and immature on another. The six dimensions are interdependent: weakness in any one creates a ceiling on what the others can achieve.

Governance maturity determines whether the program has the organizational structure to make decisions, resolve conflicts, and scale. Without governance, individual use case success does not compound into program success.
No formal AI strategy. Claude experiments run by individuals or small teams without organizational sanction. No ESC; no CoE. Decisions made ad hoc.
Claude program formally defined with executive sponsor. ESC established with at least 5 members. CoE Director in place. Decision rights documented. Phase 1 approved and underway.

Federated governance model active: BUs have defined accountability for their Claude deployments. CoE is a platform and governance body, not the only builder. ESC meets regularly and makes substantive decisions. Kill criteria defined and enforced.

AI strategy integrated with business strategy — Claude program objectives derived from company-level OKRs. Governance is self-correcting: the ESC reviews governance effectiveness and updates the model annually. External benchmarking against industry peers.
Talent maturity determines the organization's capacity to build, maintain, and improve Claude use cases. Technology does not scale without people who can use it.
No dedicated AI staff. General awareness of LLMs among some IT or data science staff. No formal training. Claude use dependent on individual motivation.

CoE team staffed (minimum: CoE Director, 1–2 AI engineers, 1 PM). Core skills present: prompt engineering, API integration, use case scoping. Basic training available for BU users (at least one workshop delivered).

Embedded AI engineers in 2+ BUs. Champion network active with trained champions in most BUs. CoE team can operate independently across Tier 1, 2, and 3 use cases. Advanced skills present: RAG architecture, fine-tuning, output evaluation.

AI skills widely distributed across the organization — not concentrated in the CoE. Internal AI training pipeline: the organization can upskill new staff without external training vendors. CoE is training other organizations in the industry (knowledge-sharing, thought leadership).

Portfolio maturity measures the breadth and depth of Claude deployment across the organization. A mature portfolio is diverse (multiple BUs, multiple use case types), well-governed (tiered, kill criteria applied), and generating measurable value.
0–1 use cases, typically in pilot or experimental status. No formal intake process. No portfolio view.
3–10 production use cases across at least 2 BUs. Formal intake and tier classification process in place. At least one Finance-reviewed ROI case.
10–30 production use cases. Tier 3 use cases in production. Kill criteria applied: at least one use case retired based on performance criteria. Portfolio review cadence established.

30+ production use cases. AI embedded in core business processes (not peripheral tasks). Use case innovation pipeline: BUs self-generate new use case ideas without CoE prompting. Annual portfolio refresh removes lowest-value use cases to make room for new ones.

Platform maturity measures the technical infrastructure that supports Claude deployment. Without shared infrastructure — API gateways, monitoring, security controls, audit logging — each use case is a one-off project with its own technical debt.
Direct API access using shared or developer API keys. No central API gateway. No audit logging. No monitoring. Each use case deployed independently.

Central API gateway with per-use-case API key management. Security controls: input/output filtering for PII and sensitive data. Basic audit logging: request/response logging with retention policy. Model version pinning.

Self-service platform: BUs can deploy Tier 1 and 2 use cases without CoE engineering involvement. RAG capabilities available as shared infrastructure. Monitoring and alerting: accuracy drift detection, anomaly detection, cost monitoring by use case.

MLOps: automated testing, deployment pipelines, and rollback for Claude-based applications. Fine-tuning infrastructure (if applicable). Custom model deployment capability. Platform used as a basis for external AI products or services.

Data maturity determines how well the organization can feed Claude with relevant, accurate, and appropriately governed information. LLMs are only as good as the context they receive — organizations with poor data maturity see consistently lower-quality Claude outputs regardless of prompt engineering quality.
No enterprise data strategy for AI. Data access is ad hoc: use case builders find and use data opportunriistically. No data classification policy for AI contexts. PII handling undefined.

Data classification policy defined: what data types can be sent to the Claude API vs. must stay on-premises. Approved internal data sources identified per use case type. Basic data lineage: know what data each use case uses.

Enterprise knowledge base available for RAG: internal documents, policies, SOPs, and knowledge articles are indexed and retrievable. Data quality standards for AI inputs: validation and preprocessing pipelines. Data governance reviews for new use cases are part of the intake process.

Real-time data pipelines: Claude use cases can access live operational data (not just batch-updated indexes). Continuous learning loops: use case performance data feeds back into data quality improvement. Data strategy and AI strategy are unified: the organization's data investments are made with AI use in mind.

Culture maturity is the most difficult to measure and the most important to change. All the technology and governance in the world does not generate value if users do not adopt Claude tools in their daily work.

Widespread skepticism or indifference about AI. Little or no user-initiated demand for Claude tools. Conversations about AI dominated by fears (job loss, data risk) rather than opportunity. No AI training available.

Early adopter community engaged: 10–20% of target users are active Claude users, mostly self-selected enthusiasts. Basic training available and attended. Executive endorsement visible. Some BU leaders actively champion AI adoption.

Majority adoption: 60%+ of target users have completed training and use at least one Claude tool monthly. AI is discussed in business reviews alongside other performance metrics. New employees are onboarded with Claude access as part of their standard onboarding.

AI-first mindset: employees default to asking 'can Claude help with this?' before executing tasks manually. Continuous experimentation norm: employees regularly try new Claude use cases informally and report findings to the CoE. AI use is a component of performance conversations in some BUs.
The Assessment Rubric: How to Score Each Dimension
The maturity assessment is conducted annually, ideally in the quarter before the program's annual ESC review. It uses a combination of data review and structured interviews to score each dimension.
Before interviews, the CoE Director assembles data for each dimension:
ESC charter, decision log, meeting attendance record, phase gate completion record
Org chart, job descriptions, training completion records, champion list, skills assessment results
Portfolio register (number, tier, status, ROI), intake log, kill/sunset log
API gateway logs, security assessment, monitoring dashboard, infrastructure documentation
Data classification policy, approved data source registry, RAG index coverage assessment
Monthly active user counts, training completion rates, satisfaction survey results, employee-generated use case ideas
Data alone cannot score culture or governance maturity. For each dimension, conduct 20-minute structured interviews with 2–3 people best positioned to assess it:
ESC Chair, CoE Director, and one BU leader
CoE technical lead, one BU champion, and one BU user
CoE PM, one BU owner of an active use case, one BU owner of a sunsetted use case
CoE technical lead, CISO, and one external developer who has used the platform
CoE Director, CDO or data engineering lead, and one use case builder who experienced data access friction
CHRO, one enthusiast user, and one reluctant or non-user (to understand resistance)
In each interview, ask the same three questions:
'Based on the rubric descriptions, which level best describes where we are today on this dimension?'
'What is the single biggest gap between where we are and the next level?'
'What would it take — specifically — to close that gap in the next 12 months?'

After interviews, the CoE Director compiles scores for each dimension. Where scores differ between interviewees, use the lowest score unless there is specific evidence that the higher score is accurate. Maturity models are more useful when they understate maturity (creating ambition) than when they overstate it (creating complacency).
The overall program maturity level is the lowest of the six dimension scores — the weakest link sets the ceiling on what the program can achieve:

# Example Maturity Assessment Output

Organization: Acme Financial Services
Assessment date: Q4 2025

Dimension                  Score   Gap to Next Level
-------------------------------------------------
1. Strategy & Governance     L3    Need: annual strategy integration
2. Talent & Capability       L2    Gap: no embedded BU engineers yet
3. Use Case Portfolio        L3    Need: more Tier 3 use cases
4. Platform & Technology     L2    Gap: no self-service platform yet
5. Data & Knowledge          L2    Gap: enterprise knowledge base not built
6. Culture & Adoption        L2    Gap: only 30% of target users active

Overall maturity level: L2 (limited by Talent, Platform, Data, Culture)

Priority investments for L2 → L3:
  1. Hire 2 embedded BU AI engineers (Talent gap)
  2. Build self-service platform (Platform gap)
  3. Launch enterprise knowledge base project (Data gap)
  4. Scale training: target 60% user completion (Culture gap)
Using the Assessment to Prioritize Investment

The maturity assessment is an investment tool, not just a diagnostic. The goal is to identify the dimension(s) holding the program back and direct investment there first — rather than advancing strong dimensions while neglecting weak ones.

the dimension furthest from the next level is the binding constraint. Investing in dimensions already close to the next level while ignoring the binding constraint is a common and costly mistake. If Culture is at L1 and Platform is at L2, investing in platform upgrades before driving adoption is building a highway no one drives on.

some dimensions must precede others. Governance must be at L2 before Data can meaningfully reach L2 (without governance, data classification policy has no enforcement). Platform must be at L3 before Culture can meaningfully reach L4 (without self-service tools, broad adoption cannot happen). The sequence is not always obvious — use the assessment interviews to surface dependencies.

after each assessment, set specific targets for each dimension for the next 12 months. These targets should be concrete and falsifiable: 'Culture: 60% of target users complete training by Q3' rather than 'Culture: improve adoption.' Present these targets to the ESC as commitments.
maturity changes slowly. Quarterly re-assessment creates busy-work without meaningful signal. Annual assessment allows enough time for genuine progress and surfaces year-on-year trends.
The organization that knows it is at L2 on Culture and invests in closing that gap is making a strategic decision. The organization that assumes it is at L3 without measuring is investing blindly.
Level Transition Criteria: What It Takes to Move Up

Identifying your current maturity level is a diagnostic. Understanding what it takes to move to the next level is the investment roadmap. For each dimension, the transition from L2 to L3 (the most common investment challenge) requires specific organizational actions:
Establish federated governance: BU-level AI leads with defined accountability. CoE shifts to platform provider role. Kill criteria documented and first sunset executed.
Hire/assign 2+ embedded BU AI engineers. Train and activate BU champions in every BU (not just 1–2). CoE team can independently build Tier 3 use cases.
Reach 10 production use cases with active users. First Tier 3 use case in production. First performance-based kill executed. Service catalog live.
Self-service platform live for Tier 1 use cases. RAG infrastructure available for use case builders. Monitoring dashboard tracking all production use cases.
Enterprise knowledge base built and indexed for RAG. Data classification policy enforced (not just documented). Data governance review part of every use case intake.
60%+ of target users complete training. New employee onboarding includes Claude access and basic training. At least 3 BUs have active Claude communities of practice.

These timelines assume full organizational commitment — dedicated investment, executive support, and no major organizational disruptions. Organizations with competing priorities, low executive engagement, or limited AI talent should extend these estimates by 50–100%.
The L1 → L2 Transition: The Hardest Step

Many organizations find the L1 → L2 transition to be the most difficult — not because L2 is technically complex, but because it requires the organization to make commitments it has previously avoided:

L1 programs run on discretionary time and free-tier API access. L2 requires a budget line item — for the CoE team, the API costs, and the infrastructure. The budget commitment is often the primary barrier to L1 → L2 transition.

L1 programs often have no clear owner — they are driven by enthusiastic individuals in various functions. L2 requires a CoE Director with authority and accountability. Naming that person forces an organizational commitment that was previously absent.

establishing the ESC requires recruiting executive time and attention at a moment when the program has not yet demonstrated the value that would justify that attention. This chicken-and-egg problem is resolved by a well-constructed investment thesis (see the Investment Thesis topic) that makes the governance commitment worthwhile before ROI is demonstrated.

L1 programs can claim any success as a win. L2 programs have committed milestones and success criteria that create the possibility of visible failure. Moving from L1 to L2 requires organizational tolerance for transparent progress tracking — not every organization has that culture.
Common Maturity Imbalances and Their Consequences
Organizations rarely advance uniformly across all six dimensions. Certain imbalance patterns are common and each creates predictable problems:
The organization has invested in sophisticated AI infrastructure — an API gateway, a self-service platform, RAG capabilities — but user adoption is low. The platform is a solution looking for users.
Symptom: low API usage despite platform availability; BUs are aware of the tools but not using them
Root cause: the organization built supply (tools) without creating demand (motivation and skills to use them)
Consequence: sunk infrastructure cost with low value return; executive skepticism about future AI investment
Correction: freeze further platform investment; launch an intensive adoption drive — champions, training, BU-specific use case workshops — before building more infrastructure
High Use Case Count, Low Governance
The organization has many Claude use cases in production, but governance is ad hoc — no tier classification, no kill criteria, no accuracy monitoring. The portfolio is large but unmanaged.
Symptom: CoE cannot report aggregate ROI or adoption; use case quality varies wildly; some use cases are producing known-bad outputs without consequence
Root cause: the program scaled use case delivery before building the governance to manage scale
Consequence: growing tail of zombie use cases consuming maintenance capacity; first significant AI incident will expose the governance gap to executive and board scrutiny

Correction: freeze new use case development for one quarter; conduct a portfolio audit — classify all existing use cases by tier, measure accuracy, and apply kill criteria retroactively to the lowest-performing cases
The CoE has skilled AI engineers who can build sophisticated Claude applications, but the organization's data is inaccessible, ungoverned, or low-quality — limiting what the engineers can build.

Symptom: use case performance consistently below expectations; engineers spending 60%+ of time on data access and quality issues rather than AI development; RAG applications producing irrelevant retrievals
Root cause: the organization hired AI talent before building the data foundations that talent needs to be effective
Consequence: talented engineers get frustrated by the inability to build quality applications and leave; the program's ROI is limited not by AI capability but by data quality
Correction: align the CoE Director and CDO on a shared data-for-AI roadmap. The CoE's highest-value investment for 6–12 months may be in data quality and accessibility, not AI development.

Users are enthusiastic about Claude and are finding their own ways to use it — but without a supported platform, they are using consumer Claude.ai, personal API accounts, or unofficial workarounds that bypass security and governance controls.

Symptom: organic Claude usage is visible on employee devices and in outputs; CISO is concerned about unauthorized data in consumer AI tools; CoE cannot account for AI use that is happening outside its program
Root cause: user demand outpaced the organization's ability to provide governed AI access
Consequence: security and data handling risk from ungoverned AI use; difficulty establishing governance norms when workarounds are already entrenched

Correction: urgent priority on deploying a governed, enterprise-grade Claude access mechanism (Claude for Teams or Claude API via a company-managed interface). Meet users where they are — don't prohibit without providing a better alternative, or the workarounds will continue underground.
Use this worksheet to conduct a self-assessment before the structured interview phase. Rate each dimension on a 1–4 scale and note the primary evidence for the score.

# Claude Maturity Self-Assessment Worksheet

Instructions: For each dimension, identify the level (1–4) where ALL
criteria are met. A dimension is at level N only if ALL criteria for
levels 1 through N are met. Circle the highest level where all criteria
are fully satisfied.

DIMENSION 1: STRATEGY & GOVERNANCE
  L1 criteria (must all be true):
    □ Claude is being used somewhere in the organization
    □ At least one person is aware of what Claude can do

  L2 criteria (all L1 + all of these):
    □ Executive sponsor identified and committed
    □ ESC established with at least 5 members and quarterly cadence
    □ CoE Director in role with defined scope
    □ Decision rights documented
    □ Phase 1 budget approved

  L3 criteria (all L2 + all of these):
    □ Federated governance: BU AI leads with accountability
    □ CoE operating as platform/governance body (not only builder)
    □ Kill criteria defined, documented, and applied to at least 1 use case
    □ Service catalog operational with SLAs

  L4 criteria (all L3 + all of these):
    □ AI strategy formally integrated into company strategic plan
    □ Annual governance effectiveness review by ESC
    □ External benchmarking against industry peers

Score: ___ / 4     Primary evidence: ___________________

---

(Repeat format for Dimensions 2–6)

---

OVERALL ASSESSMENT
  Individual dimension scores:
    D1 Strategy & Governance:   ___
    D2 Talent & Capability:     ___
    D3 Use Case Portfolio:      ___
    D4 Platform & Technology:   ___
    D5 Data & Knowledge:        ___
    D6 Culture & Adoption:      ___

  Overall maturity level (lowest score): ___

  Binding constraint (dimension furthest from next level): ___

  Primary investment priorities for next 12 months:
    1. _______________________________________
    2. _______________________________________
    3. _______________________________________
Communicating Maturity Results to the ESC
The maturity assessment results should be presented to the ESC as the basis for the following year's investment plan. The presentation should:
Show current scores on all six dimensions with the evidence base (not just assertion)
Highlight the binding constraint clearly — the dimension that is setting the ceiling on overall maturity
Present the investment required to address the binding constraint, with estimated cost and timeline
Connect the maturity target to the program's phase plan: 'To complete Phase 2, we need to reach L3 on Talent and Platform. Here is what that requires.'
Ask the ESC to approve the maturity investment plan, not just acknowledge the assessment

A maturity assessment that produces a report but no investment decision is a diagnostic without a treatment. The ESC meeting where results are presented should close with a specific, funded plan to address the binding constraint.
Industry Benchmarks: Where Does Your Program Stand?
Maturity assessments are more useful when contextualized against industry peers. While organization-specific data varies, broad patterns from enterprise AI program research suggest:
Strategy & Governance (no ESC, no formal program)
Culture & Adoption (tools exist, users not adopting)
Data & Knowledge (platform built, data not accessible)
Culture & Adoption (most users trained, top 20% driving 80% of value)

These are central tendencies, not predictions for any specific organization. Financial services and healthcare organizations typically lag on Governance and Data dimensions due to regulatory complexity. Technology-native organizations typically lead on Platform and Talent. Consumer-facing organizations often lead on Culture (external AI experience transfers to internal use) but lag on Governance.

The Claude adoption maturity model defines six dimensions — Strategy and Governance, Talent and Capability, Use Case Portfolio, Platform and Technology, Data and Knowledge, and Culture and Adoption — each with four levels from Initial (L1) to Optimizing (L4). The overall maturity level is the lowest of the six (weakest link). The assessment combines data review with structured interviews, scored conservatively. The outputs are used to identify the binding constraint — the dimension furthest from the next level — and sequence investments accordingly. Reassess annually and present results to the ESC as the basis for investment prioritization.
